# Transcrição: 03-First Last Best Worst

- **Fonte:** 03-First Last Best Worst
- **Duração:** 15:16
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Okay, you're doing homework for life. Good job. Now, it's time to recover some memories from the past, to find moments in our lives that maybe we have left behind that we get to recapture. We're going to play a game called First, Last, Best, Worst. Take a look at this worksheet here. You're going to be filling it in for yourself. It's a very simple game. It's predicated on the idea that the first and the last times we do something are often very story worthy. Also, the best and the worst experiences of our lives also tend to be very story worthy. So what I'm going to do is I'm going to go through a series of prompts and I want you to play along with me. We're going to play the game together. I'll show you how it works and then I'll actually play the game live in the next video for you so you can see what it looks like in real time. But let's play this game together. So as you can see on the worksheet, the first prompt is the word kiss. And so in your worksheet, what you would think about is when was the first time you were kissed? For me, it was Laura. It was in the stairwell of a hotel in Pasadena, California. And so in my square, I'll write Laura. I know what that means. My last kiss was Charlie, my nine year old son. He kissed me before I left the house today. The best kiss I ever had. Well, that kiss from Laura in Pasadena was pretty fantastic. So I would list that one. But I guess I should also list the first kiss from my wife, Alicia, which happened in a parking lot unexpected. She leaned into my car and she kissed me because I had not kissed her soon enough. That was pretty great too. So you get to write as many things as you want in the boxes. Now if you're writing more than one first kiss, you had a very interesting first kiss. Typically that's going to be one person. But your best kiss, maybe you're like me. Maybe you've had a bunch. And what was your worst kiss? Mine was Sheila. Terrible kisser. I broke up with her because she was a terrible kisser and I was a terrible person. Maybe you can't remember your worst kiss. Maybe you've never had a worst kiss. Lucky you. You can also leave boxes empty. That's perfectly fine. So fill in all of your kiss boxes. Next we'll move to teacher. Who was your very first teacher? Mine was Mrs. Dubois, my kindergarten teacher. Then your last teacher. My last teacher was a professor at Central Connecticut State University. I took a class with him. My best teacher? This box is going to be filled with a few names. Patrick Sullivan, Jackie LeBlanc, Mr. Moroney, Mrs. Schultz. All of my best teachers.

## [03:00 - 06:01]

And then your worst teacher. For me, Mr. Moran, sixth grade science. Fill in your teachers now. The next one is pet. What was your first pet? You already know my first pet, Measelman. The dog named after the doctor who gave my father his vasectomy. What was your last pet? If you have a pet right now, that's probably your last pet. For me it's Toby and Pluto, my cats. Your best pet? For me, you know that too. Kaylee. And my worst pet, Prudence, a cat that used to attack us all the time as children. Fill in yours right now. Your first pet, your last pet, your best pet, and your worst pet. Next one, injury. What was the very first injury that you can remember? For me, I split my head open when I was about two years old. My parents don't know how I did it. I ended up with stitches on my forehead in the shape of a cross. I don't remember it happening, but I do know that that was my first injury. My last injury? I had surgery and I'm having a problem with my leg as a result. I'm having that problem right now as I record this video. My best injury, which means the injury that happened to you that also resulted in something kind of fantastic. For me, it was going through the windshield of a Dotson B210 on a snowy day, December 23, 1988. It was one of those days when my heart stopped beating and I stopped breathing and a paramedic used CPR to restore my life. It was a terrible, terrible day, but it was also the day I discovered that my friends were like my family. And then your worst injury, which for me is that same day, going through that windshield of that Dotson B210, because sometimes you can have the same thing in more than one box. Fill in your first, last, best, and worst injuries now. The next one is boss. Who is your first boss ever? For me, Jesse Deacon, a man who owned a farm and I was a farmhand when I was a boy. Your last boss, for me, it's the principal who runs the school where I work now, Scott Dunn. My best boss, again there'll be a couple here, Plato-Carafellas, a principal who used to run the school, Jalou Montessere, a manager at a McDonald's where I once worked. Those are my best bosses ever. Hopefully you have one.

## [06:01 - 09:09]

I've met people who have never had a best boss. If that's the case, I'm sorry, leave a blank. And then your worst boss. I won't mention his name here. I'll just use his initials, J.S. Absolutely my worst boss ever. Maybe you have more than one if you do, I'm sorry. Fill it in now. Next one, friend. Who is the first friend you can remember having? For me, it was a boy named Peter. Who is the last friend you can remember having? Which might be the last new friend that you made. The last new friend who I made is a man named Mark. Last friend, maybe you have more than one. I do. I would list Benji. I would list Jeff. I would list David. I would list Danny. All of those best friends. I'd probably put Laura on the list too. And maybe even Alicia. And then your worst friend, which might be your frenemy, might be the friend you have that makes your life difficult. For me, maybe a guy named Eric. Yeah, Eric. Eric is probably my worst friend right now. Good. You've filled in your boxes. You've completed your first, last, best, worst worksheet. Here's the next step. What I want you to do is to go through that worksheet now, pause this video, go through it, and I want you to circle any one of those items in those boxes that you could tell a story about. And what I mean by a story is I could speak about this item, this person, this thing for 20 seconds in a slightly interesting way that qualifies as a story in this game. All the way up to I could speak for 20 minutes about this thing and still have stuff to say. So the bar is low. I'm not looking for a fully realized, emotionally moving, suspenseful story. Just I can talk about it a little bit for a little bit of time and it might be interesting to someone. You're going to go through all of the items, put some thought into it. Ask yourself, is there something I could say about this that would fill a little bit of time and maybe be entertaining or interesting or unique? And if so, circle it. Go ahead and do that now.

## [09:12 - 12:17]

Okay. How did you do? How many circles do you have? I know you have at least one. I suspect you have many. If you start doing homework for life, you'll have even more because you'll begin to see stories where you can't see them yet. For me, almost every single item is circled. I can find something slightly interesting to say about every one of them, not because I'm special, but because I've been doing homework for life for about a decade and I see stories where other people don't see them. If I took your list, I sat down with you and I started asking you questions about each one of the items. I suspect we would find a story about every single one of them. But here's the important question. Did you recover a memory from the past? Did you find something that you had forgotten before, but it is now back with you? If so, that's the point of the game. The point is, we find things from the past that we have forgotten. We bring them to the present and now we have new things to talk about. Now I gave you some prompts that are pretty easy. Those are prompts that I use in workshops all the time and they tend to generate really story worthy ideas. But this game works no matter what word you use. I said this in a workshop years ago and a guy in the back, one of these guys that sort of sits in the back with his arms folded, you wonder why he's even taken your workshop. He never looks happy. From the back of the room, he said, anything? And I thought, oh really? You're here? And so I said to him, yes, sir, anything. Give me any word right now and I'll play first, last, best, worst for you. And he said tree. He said tree like tree was going to be a hard prompt. I remember thinking in my head, tree. You could have used chandelier. You could have used potpourri. You gave me tree. So I said to him tree. Okay, sir. Here we go. First tree, the first tree that I can remember, the oak tree at the bottom of my driveway as a child that I stood under while waiting for the bus. I could tell you 10 stories about things that happened under that oak tree and things about

## [12:17 - 15:16]

that oak tree in particular, piece of cake. That was my first tree. My last tree, at least the last tree that I had of any meaning. I used to have two trees in my front yard. My daughter Clara named them Emma and Sarah. Emma became diseased and we had to have an arborist come out and remove Emma from the front yard. My daughter sat on the steps and cried while they removed Emma. And then she would go and stand in the spot that Emma used to be and she would tell my son Charlie stories about the time. Emma used to provide her shade and comfort and now she's gone. You can see that that would be a story. That was the last tree. The best tree of my life. A few years ago we had a snowstorm in October in New England that knocked down a lot of trees because they still had the leaves on them. One of the trees in my backyard fell down and my friend Tom came over to chop it up for me. Tom is a wonderful person but full of hubris. So as he goes out with his chainsaw to cut the tree, I say, hey, do you want me to hold that tree steady for you? And he says, nah, I got it. And then as he steps up onto the tree with the chainsaw, he slips and he nearly takes his own leg off with the chainsaw. And I remember thinking, that tree just taught you a lesson, buddy. That's the best tree I've ever seen. A tree that taught a guy about humility. And then the worst tree, my great-great-grandmother planted a row of pine trees behind my grandfather's house. And they grew up tall. And when I was a kid, I used to climb them all the time. And one day I fell out of the tree and I broke my ankle. And I remember lying on the ground and the thought that went through my childhood mind was, a long time ago, my great-great-grandmother planted this tree and now it's broken my leg. That was the worst tree ever. That was tree. I told that guy I could tell a story about all of them. Any prompt will work. It won't work as well as kiss. It won't work as well as pet. It won't work as well as friend. But any prompt, including something as simple as tree, will work for you. So this is the start of your first-last, best-worst journey. This is an activity that I do when I find myself in a meeting where someone thinks I need to be in the meeting but I know I don't need to be in the meeting. I do a first-last, best-worst. I look around the room. I find an object. I say I've never used that object before. And I do a first-last, best-worst. This is the kind of activity that I do when I'm driving with a friend to a place far away. I'll say, hey, let's play first, last, best, worst, tell each other stories. It's a wonderful way to accumulate stories from your past. Bring them to the present. And then what I do, I enter them into my homework for life as memories. They get entered in there as well so I can hold on to them forever. In the next video, I'm going to play a live version of first, last, best, worst to show you how this can work.
